authoritative_sources.title: Medicare Benefit Policy Manual, Chapter 1 - Inpatient Hospital Services Covered Under Part A
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS is the Medicare payer and policy administrator. Chapter 1 directly supports inpatient admission-order evidence, timing, ordering-practitioner boundaries, and ambiguous order handling for hospital admissions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: admission order, inpatient status evidence, and admission-readiness review

authoritative_sources.title: 42 CFR 482.24 - Condition of participation: Medical record services
authoritative_sources.publisher: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations / Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Hospital Conditions of Participation define medical-record obligations for hospital services. The section grounds admissions evidence expectations around orders, documentation, and the patient record.
used_for: missing or conflicting record failure modes

authoritative_sources.title: 42 CFR 482.30 - Condition of participation: Utilization review
authoritative_sources.publisher: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations / Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Hospital Conditions of Participation define utilization-review governance for admissions and continued stays, making it authoritative for routing medical necessity and admission-status questions away from the admissions intake lane.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: utilization review boundary and admission-status escalation

authoritative_sources.title: 42 CFR 482.43 - Condition of participation: Discharge planning
authoritative_sources.publisher: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations / Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Hospital Conditions of Participation define discharge planning and transfer obligations. They support the boundary between admissions handoff evidence and case-management or discharge-planning authority.

authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Eligibility Transaction System (HETS) 270/271
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS operates the Medicare real-time eligibility transaction channel. It directly supports eligibility evidence, 270/271 terminology, and the boundary between coverage evidence and admissions clearance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility verification and coverage evidence capture
used_for: eligibility versus referral, authorization, order, or placement boundary

authoritative_sources.title: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule (CMS-0057-F)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS-0057-F is federal public policy for impacted payer prior authorization processes, APIs, status transparency, and denial-reason communication. It supports authorization status handling without granting approval authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: authorization status readiness and payer-response handling

authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Minimum Necessary Standard
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR is the HIPAA privacy regulator. Minimum-necessary guidance directly constrains admissions status requests, transfer packets, payer authorization details, and escalation notes containing PHI.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-gated admissions evidence packaging and requester handling

authoritative_sources.title: HL7 FHIR R4 Encounter Resource
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HL7 is a recognized healthcare interoperability standards body. The Encounter resource grounds vendor-neutral encounter class, status, hospitalization, admit source, and discharge disposition terminology for ADT and EHR evidence mapping.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ADT, encounter, and admissions terminology normalization
used_for: admit source and hospitalization artifact mapping

authoritative_sources.title: National Patient Safety Goals
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Joint Commission is a recognized healthcare accrediting body. Its patient-identification safety goals support refusal to clear admission or placement when identity, encounter, or demographic evidence conflicts.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient identity reconciliation and safety escalation

workflow_questions.question: What prerequisites or authorizations gate the workflow?
workflow_questions.answer: Minimum prerequisites include confirmed patient/member identity, MRN or MPI match, encounter or pre-admit shell, requested admission type, admit source, facility, service line, requested service date or arrival window, admitting or attending practitioner, admission or observation order status, bed or placement request, payer and plan context, eligibility response, referral dependency, authorization status, utilization review intake path where applicable, consent/privacy posture, tenant source hierarchy, retrieval hooks, and queue owner.

workflow_questions.question: Which records or systems establish the patient, member, or claim truth set?
workflow_questions.answer: Truth-set systems include EHR/EMR and clinical documentation, ADT and encounter systems, registration and MPI, order entry, bed board or placement systems, eligibility transaction systems, payer portals, authorization and referral platforms, transfer center systems, utilization review and case management systems, document repositories, patient/member access or CRM systems, consent/privacy systems, and audit logs. Tenant source hierarchy determines which record controls when systems conflict.

workflow_questions.question: Where does the lane stop and licensed clinical, privacy, or payer-interpretation authority begin?
workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops before admission approval, diagnosis, treatment planning, clinical urgency, level-of-care selection, isolation or placement safety judgment, medical necessity, utilization review determination, authorization approval, payer policy interpretation, privacy-release approval, legal interpretation, financial approval, coding, claim correction, denial appeal, discharge planning, or care-transition decisioning. Those require adjacent owner escalation or orchestrator return.

workflow_stages.name: Admissions intake and lane classification
workflow_stages.description: Normalize request intent, admission type, admit source, facility, service line, service date or arrival window, patient/member identity, encounter shell, order status, payer, plan, bed request, and desired action before treating work as actionable.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Identity, encounter, and order readiness
workflow_stages.description: Validate MRN, MPI match, demographics, encounter or pre-admit shell, ADT posture, admission or observation order, ordering practitioner context, and documentation trail against controlling source systems.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Eligibility, referral, authorization, and utilization review prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Check current eligibility, payer and plan context, referral requirements, authorization status, payer portal evidence, service setting, facility, service line, and whether utilization review or payer-facing owner action is required before admission readiness.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance

workflow_stages.name: Bed, placement, transfer, and documentation blocker classification
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile ADT, EHR, bed-board, transfer center, registration, order, payer portal, authorization, referral, utilization review, case management, privacy, and audit records; classify blockers as identity, order, encounter, placement, eligibility, authorization, referral, utilization review, documentation, privacy, source conflict, or tenant policy related.
source_classes: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Disposition, routing, and controlled escalation
workflow_stages.description: Produce accept, pend, reject, route, or blocked dispositions; create admission-readiness summaries and escalation packets; route PHI, patient-safety, clinical, payer, privacy, utilization-review, revenue-cycle, discharge, and source-conflict cases to responsible owners.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

artifacts: Patient demographics, MRN, MPI match evidence, date of birth, legal name, contact details, guarantor, patient/member status, and patient communication preferences
Encounter or pre-admit shell, ADT status, admission request, admit source, encounter class, service line, facility, service date or arrival window, bed request, bed class, and placement queue state
Admission or observation order, ordering/referring/admitting/attending practitioner details, clinical-administrative notes, transfer packet, and utilization review intake flag

Insurance card images, payer and plan identifiers, subscriber/member IDs, eligibility 270/271 responses, coverage dates, referral records, authorization records, authorization numbers, payer portal statuses, and attachment acknowledgements
Bed-management notes, transfer center case IDs, case management notes, discharge or care-transition dependencies, referral packets, document repository records, and audit logs
Consent, proxy, privacy restriction, minimum-necessary handling, disclosure, patient-location or admission-status request, and patient communication records
Intake disposition notes, admission prerequisite checklists, eligibility or authorization blocker summaries, placement-readiness summaries, routing summaries, and escalation packets
systems_of_record: EHR/EMR and associated clinical documentation systems
ADT, encounter, registration, and master patient index systems
Bed board, patient placement, transfer center, and capacity management systems
Real-time eligibility and benefits verification systems
Payer portals and electronic prior-authorization platforms
Utilization review and case management systems
Practice management, revenue cycle, charge capture, coding, claim, remittance, denial, and appeal systems for routing context only
Document repository, fax-import, attachment, CRM, patient/member access, patient communication, consent, privacy, identity-access, and audit systems

allowed: Classify the admissions request as accepted, pended, rejected, routed, or blocked based on documented patient, encounter, payer, provider, facility, order, authorization, referral, placement, privacy, and tenant-policy evidence.

Determine whether administrative admission or placement readiness can proceed based on identity, encounter, order, eligibility, referral, authorization, placement, privacy, and documentation prerequisites.

Identify whether the blocker is identity, demographic, order, encounter, ADT, bed/placement, transfer, eligibility, referral, authorization, utilization-review handoff, documentation, privacy, source-conflict, or tenant-policy related.
Prepare intake disposition notes, prerequisite checklists, eligibility or authorization blocker summaries, placement-readiness summaries, and escalation packets with source traceability.
escalate: Tenant payer, plan, facility, service-line, bed class, source hierarchy, retrieval hook, jurisdiction, privacy, or queue-owner rules are missing and would change admissions disposition.
EHR, ADT, registration, order, bed board, payer portal, eligibility, referral, authorization, transfer center, utilization review, case management, privacy, or audit systems materially conflict.

The request involves PHI disclosure uncertainty, proxy or consent ambiguity, patient-safety risk, patient identity mismatch, clinical urgency, level-of-care selection, isolation handling, medical necessity, payer policy interpretation, or authorization approval.
Public baseline guidance and tenant policy, payer contract, facility rule, state program requirement, or bed-management rule materially conflict.
refuse: Accept, reserve, admit, or clear placement despite unresolved identity, encounter, order, eligibility, referral, authorization, privacy, consent, placement, documentation, or tenant-policy prerequisites.
Diagnose, triage, determine clinical urgency, choose treatment, approve inpatient status, select level of care, assign isolation precautions, or state that admission is medically necessary.
Approve or deny authorization, interpret payer policy as binding, override a payer prerequisite, or write appeal or claim strategy.

Disclose admission status, patient location, authorization details, or transfer packet PHI when requester authority, minimum-necessary purpose, consent, proxy status, or communication rule is unresolved.
Take ownership of coding, charge capture, claim correction, denial cleanup, appeal strategy, discharge planning, or post-acute provider selection.

domain_failure_modes: admission or bed placement is accepted even though identity, encounter, order, eligibility, referral, authorization, or tenant prerequisites remain missing
active eligibility is treated as full admission clearance when inpatient authorization, referral, order, or utilization-review prerequisites are unresolved
ambiguous observation or inpatient order language is converted into inpatient admission readiness without practitioner or utilization-review clarification
wrong-patient or wrong-encounter admission occurs because MRN, MPI, ADT, EHR, registration, and payer conflicts are guessed away
PHI such as admission status, unit location, authorization detail, or transfer packet content is disclosed without verified authority and minimum-necessary handling
administrative admissions intake is converted into clinical admission, level-of-care, isolation, or medical-necessity judgment
payer portal, eligibility, referral, authorization, EHR, ADT, and bed-board statuses conflict and the lane resolves them without tenant source hierarchy
direct admission or transfer proceeds without accepting service, attending practitioner, bed class, transfer center case ID, arrival window, or authorization evidence
post-service coding, charge, claim, remittance, denial, appeal, discharge planning, or care-transition work is misrouted into a pre-execution admissions lane
generic intake language omits hospital-specific ADT, encounter, order, bed-board, utilization-review, payer, facility, service-line, and documentation details that materially change the answer

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Missing authorization, referral, or eligibility dependency
PHI or privacy-sensitive request that requires controlled handling
Boundary case that would require licensed clinical or payer-interpretation escalation
Missing prerequisite or required intake data
Request routed to the wrong lane before execution starts
Attempt to bypass access, eligibility, or authorization checks
Invalid, incomplete, or ineligible admissions request
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling requires escalation
Tenant-specific adaptation preserves the public baseline
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: payer-specific inpatient authorization, referral, and notification rules
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The validated public baseline requires tenant payer, plan, product, facility, provider type, service line, source hierarchy, and contract facts before final admissions disposition. Missing tenant facts trigger pend, route, or orchestrator return rather than guessed clearance.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: facility-specific bed-management, direct-admit, transfer-center, and service-line rules
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack identifies bed and transfer dependencies but requires tenant facility policy, bed-board source hierarchy, transfer center workflow, accepting-service rules, and escalation SLAs before producing final readiness.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: state, program, and facility privacy or patient-location disclosure rules
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack applies HIPAA minimum-necessary baseline and requires tenant privacy, consent, proxy, directory, communication, and jurisdictional rules before disclosure or patient-location handling.

research_to_spec_constraints: Active eligibility alone is never sufficient to clear admission when order, referral, authorization, encounter, placement, privacy, or tenant prerequisites remain unresolved.
Inpatient-versus-observation ambiguity is a blocker requiring practitioner, utilization-review, or tenant-policy clarification; the lane may not infer clinical or payer status.
Patient identity, encounter, ADT, and demographic conflicts are safety blockers, not clerical details to smooth over.
Payer portal or public guidance status is not equivalent to authorization approval or medical-necessity determination.
PHI handling is minimum-necessary and requester-authority constrained, including admission status, unit location, authorization details, and transfer packets.

Direct-admit, transfer, and bed-placement readiness require accepting service, attending practitioner, arrival window, bed or placement facts, and owner routing without taking bed-control or clinical authority.
The runtime strategy remains specification-based until a separate trusted implementation validates connectors, PHI controls, and tenant retrieval hooks.
